data.name: Eloy Room
data.description: It was during the 2014-2015 season when Eloy Room received a phone call from Patrick Kluivert, the then Curaçao head coach, who invited the goalkeeper to represent the Blue Wave. Many other-Dutch born players were to follow. The keeper, who is known for his sharp reflexes, has experienced many memorable moments on international duty. Take the day Lionel Messi asked for his shirt. While most players have to chase the Argentinian’s shirt, on this occasion it was Messi himself – who had just scored a hat-trick against Room – who approached the goalkeeper. “When the match was finished, he was basically standing right in front of me and made a gesture of swapping shirts,” Room recalls. It was possibly to do with the fact Messi had just scored his 100th goal for Argentina and wanted Room’s shirt as a memory of the milestone but he did compliment the goalkeeper for “some good saves” during the match.

data.name: Shurandy Sambo
data.description: Most people would have given up if they had suffered a second anterior cruciate ligament tear at just 19 – but not Sambo. Remarkably, he drew inspiration from his first injury layoff. “During my second rehab, I still had the notes from the first one. I kept comparing the numbers, always trying to do better – even if it was just rowing 15 metres further,” he told Sportnieuws. Born in Geldrop in the Netherlands and developed in the PSV Eindhoven academy, Sambo grew up in a sporting family. “My brother did boxing and my sister did gymnastics,” he says. “My father played football in Curaçao, so for him it’s extra-special that we’ve now qualified for the World Cup.” His father also played a key role in his development. “I always trained extra with him,” says the full-back, who combines technical ability with strength in one-v-ones and a constant drive to push forward.

data.name: Jurién Gaari
data.description: After starting out in the Dutch amateur game, he and a teammate came up with an original idea: they were going to email professional clubs in search of an opportunity. “We drafted a message at his house, looked up email addresses on club websites, and then it was copy and paste,” he told Voetbal International. “Sometimes we got a reply, often we didn’t.” The approach paid off, though, as Gaari was invited to train with Excelsior Rotterdam and eventually earned a place in the academy. After several years there, he returned to the amateur game. It was during his time at Kozakken Boys that his international journey began. After a match against BVV Barendrecht, his teammate Raily Ignacio informed him that the then Curaçao manager, Remko Bicentini, was waiting outside. The two had a discussion and Garri was suddenly an international player. He was part of Curaçao’s Caribbean Cup win and first ever Gold Cup participation in 2017 – still as an amateur. Has been playing in Saudi Arabia since 2024 and is currently with the second-tier side Abha.

data.name: Roshon van Eijma
data.description: When Van Eijma signed his first professional terms with Roda JC, he picked up an extra job delivering pizzas to top up his wages. “Sometimes I’d turn up at someone’s door with a pizza and they’d recognise me from a match. It took a bit of getting used to at first, but it’s funny too,” the centre-back told Elf Voetbal. He had to be creative, just like in his early days at Roda, where he started on €150 a month, making ends meet with help from his agent and parents. “The club gave us a studio to live in. I stayed there with other players, it felt more like a student house,” he says. A physically strong, commanding presence at the back for Curaçao, Van Eijma played for Preussen Münster and Top Oss before landing at RKC Waalwijk in 2024.

data.name: Sherel Floranus
data.description: Having represented several Dutch youth national teams, he switched allegiance to Curaçao in 2023. The decision quickly paid off, as World Cup qualification was secured after a 0–0 draw in Jamaica last year, sparking what he described as “really the best party ever”. Afterwards, footage emerged of teammates attempting to wake him by using a horn on the flight from Jamaica to Curaçao. “I knew it was going to be a long day so I told everyone I was going to sleep for an hour,” Floranus told ESPN. That did not work out but he had another opportunity to catch up on his sleep. “Luckily I managed nine hours on the flight back to the Netherlands,” he added. A cousin of the former Newcastle United defender Jetro Willems, Floranus is a versatile player, capable of playing at right-back, left-back and centre-back.

data.name: Godfried Roemeratoe
data.description: When Roemeratoe was 14, he left his family home in Zeeland to join the Twente academy in the east of the Netherlands. In his spare time, he spent endless hours on buses and trains to make the commute back home, where his brother Renzo was playing for Sparta Rotterdam. Technically gifted and physically strong, Roemeratoe broke through at Twente and, after a loan spell at Willem II, played for the Israeli side Hapoel Tel Aviv. During that time, he made his bow for Curaçao, where his second match was against the newly crowned world champions, Argentina. “Playing against Lionel Messi was something I never even dared to dream of,” Roemeratoe told Omroep Zeeland, where he recalled the moment when he was warming up and the Argentina No 10 stepped on to the pitch. “It honestly felt like I was in a movie, but then you realise it’s real and you actually see him walking around there. That was surreal.”

data.name: Juninho Bacuna
data.description: The brother of Leandro, Juninho ensures that the Bacuna family is well represented in the squad while their elder sibling, Johnsen, played at a very good amateur level. The brothers achieved a special feat in 2015 when they all played in cup finals. Juninho (Groningen) and Johnsen (Harkemase Boys) won their games against PEC Zwolle and Staphorst while Leandro (Aston Villa) lost to Arsenal. They all like to play with the No 7, although Leandro opted for the No 10 jersey for Curaçao. Their father, John, also played about 30 games for the Netherlands Antilles, from which Curaçao separated in 2010. The well-travelled and versatile Juninho left Groningen for the Premier League and Huddersfield in 2018 and played for Rangers and Birmingham before stints in Saudi Arabia, Turkey and the Netherlands.

data.name: Livano Comenencia
data.description: Not long after scoring one of the most crucial goals in Curaçao’s World Cup qualification campaign, Livano Comenencia made sure the moment would be preserved for future generations. Having scored the opener in the pivotal 2-0 win over Jamaica he went to the Fifa museum in Zurich – where he plays his club football – to donate his match shirt from the game. “Livano’s shirt represents a fresh and incredible chapter in the global game," the Fifa museum director, Marco Fazzone, said. Much like Curaçao’s journey, Comenencia’s career has already included a wide range of experiences, with a solid education at PSV and the Next Gen team of Juventus, before settling in Switzerland.

data.name: Jürgen Locadia
data.description: When Locadia joined Brighton from PSV for £15m in 2018 he looked set for a long and successful Premier League career. However, it didn’t quite work out like that and he left the south-coast club on a free four years – and several loans – later. He played in Iran, China and Spain before suddenly finding himself without a club after the 2024-25 season. A few months later, before Curaçao’s crucial World Cup qualifier against Jamaica, he received a phone call from the national team manager, Wouter Jansen. “He wasn’t in the squad at first, but then Gervane Kastaneer got injured and I knew Jürgen had trained for himself,” Jansen recalls. “We were on our way from Bermuda to Jamaica and [the head coach] Dick Advocaat said: ‘Give him a call, he can join us.’ He then started that game and did a really good job in our 0-0 draw, which qualified us for the World Cup.” It fits Locadia’s remarkable story of the last few years, including fleeing Iran during the Mahsa Amini protests and being served by robots in a restaurant in China.

data.name: Leandro Bacuna
data.description: One of the squad’s most capped – and experienced – players as he chose to represent Curaçao back in 2016, while he was still playing in the Premier League with Aston Villa. Patrick Kluivert was the head coach then and Bacuna followed in the footsteps of the goalkeeper Eloy Room, who had opted to play for Curaçao the year before. The team became incredibly close-knit, something reflected in how they honour Jairzinho Pieter, a former national-team goalkeeper who died while travelling with the squad. Pieter was known for leading the team’s daily prayers – a ritual still observed today, with Bacuna placing a necklace belonging to him at the centre of the team huddle. “His passing was very difficult at the time and it is still very difficult,” Room said. “That made the dream of reaching the World Cup come even more to life, because it was also truly his dream. It gave us even more motivation.”

data.name: Jeremy Antonisse
data.description: When Curaçao qualified for the World Cup after drawing 0-0 in Jamaica, Antonisse must have been particularly relieved. In injury time he gave away a penalty for a challenge on Jamaica’s Dujuan Richards – or so he thought, before it was overturned after a video assistant referee intervention. Moments later the final whistle sounded and he could join his teammates in the celebrations, which would continue into the following day when the squad was honoured for several hours on an open-top bus through Willemstad. The creative winger, who can also be deployed as an attacking midfielder, spent more than 10 years at the PSV academy. After a loan spell at Emmen, he moved to Moreirense in Portugal, before signing for Greek side Kifisia last year.

data.name: Sontje Hansen
data.description: The last time the Netherlands won the Under-17 Euros, in 2019, Hansen led the line and scored in the final against Italy (4–2). He was seen as a huge prospect in the Ajax academy, but it did not quite happen for him in Amsterdam so he moved to NEC Nijmegen, where he impressed to the degree that Middlesbrough gave him a four-year contract. It was Dick Advocaat who convinced him to represent Curaçao and he scored on his second appearance in a 7–0 World Cup qualifying win away to Bermuda. He is now preparing for his first major tournament with Curaçao, with his cousin Ar’jany Martha also part of the setup.

data.name: Tyrese Noslin
data.description: Noslin knows all about how quickly things can change. At the end of the 2023-24 season, he was told that he would not be able to continue playing for the Almere City reserves in the Tweede Divisie was not an option. He then decided to take things into his own hands and approached the manager of Telstar, Anthony Correia. “Tyrese suddenly sent me a message,” Correia told Voetbal International. “We then invited him for a trial at Telstar. He quickly proved to be the kind of player I like: a boy with tireless commitment. And a really nice lad. But he still needed to improve in terms of playing quality. So we started to work on that.” They did just that and Noslin helped Telstar win promotion to the Eredivisie before scoring the winner on his home debut for Curaçao in a 3-2 win against Bermuda.

data.name: Kenji Gorré
data.description: The career of Gorré shows how setbacks can lead to growth. After spending more than a decade in the Manchester United academy and later playing for Swansea, he became addicted to gambling. “I went once, then twice, and suddenly I realised: 'Hang on a second, nowadays I go every day,'” he told Voetbal International. Gorré realised he had a problem and worked on it, a process that eventually inspired him to help others, via a mindset coaching platform, where he and other footballers were able to openly discuss mental health challenges and life beyond the pitch. On the pitch, he rebuilt his career, spending five years in Portugal, and is on the books of Maccabi Haifa. The technically gifted winger has been a member of the Curaçao national team, where his father, Dean Gorré, is the assistant coach, since 2019.

data.name: Ar'jany Martha
data.description: Nurtured as a winger in the Ajax academy, Martha was suddenly moved to left-back during a season with the reserves – a switch that eventually led to his first-team debut. He adapted quickly and played his first five games in the Eredivisie with the senior side in that position. “I find playing left-back pretty OK,” he said with a smile after a match against Heerenveen. “The difficult thing is you drop back two lines. In the beginning, I found it a bit difficult.” After speaking to his coach, family and friends, he was advised to “just switch the button and do your thing there” and says he eventually felt “I’ve got it in me”. Having developed he moved to Beerschot in 2024 and now plays in League One with Rotherham United.

data.name: Jearl Margaritha
data.description: Margaritha knew that hard work was required to make it in professional football. In the end he had to be patient as well. After being released from the Groningen and Emmen academies he dropped into amateur football with VV Hoogeveen at the age of 19. “It was a difficult period, but I thought: 'OK, if I really want this, I have to show that I stand out at this level and that I want to move up,'” he told the Keuken Kampioen Divisie website. Within six months, he had earned a move to Almere City. He stayed on track with spells at Top Oss and Sabah before a move to Phoenix Rising in 2024. A creative winger with sharp acceleration, this season Margaritha joined Beveren, where he played a key role in promotion with seven goals and 12 assists. The title was already secured on 22 February, making it the earliest promotion in the Belgian second tier’s history.

data.name: Brandley Kuwas
data.description: Nine years ago Kuwas became the subject of a song bearing his name, produced by his close friend and rapper Jonna Fraser. The song was a tribute to their friendship, detailing how they spent their youth together, sharing dreams of becoming an artist and a footballer. They both went on to achieve those goals and when the song was released, Kuwas was flying high with Heracles Almelo in the Eredivisie. He even got a phone call from Danny Blind, the then Netherlands head coach, saying he was being monitored for a call-up. In the end he was not selected and shortly afterwards he chose to represent Curaçao. He has ended up wearing orange at club level though, playing for Volendam, where he started his career.

data.name: Armando Obispo
data.description: Joining PSV at a young age he played together with Cody Gakpo (now at Liverpool) and Jordan Teze (Monaco) among others. Hugely talented. he had several injury problems but came back stronger each time. He is now a reliable centre-back for the first team and has won the league three years in a row and gained valuable Champions League experience. Having represented the Netherlands at every youth level from under-15 upwards, a route to the senior team looked within reach, but the call never came and he opted to play for Curaçao, the country of his father. Obispo stands out for his strength in one-v-ones and his buildup play. “He’s also an excellent header of the ball and has an outstanding left foot,” says his PSV manager, Peter Bosz.

data.name: Gervane Kastaneer
data.description: Kastaneer was a promising talent at ADO Den Haag and had already signed a pre-contract at the German side Mainz when disaster struck during a game against PEC Zwolle. Shortly after opening the scoring, the ball struck his eye, causing a torn retina. The team doctor immediately took him off but Kastaneer was not happy and said: “Come on, man, I can just play with one eye, or not?” – believing he had only got pitch pellets in his eye. The injury turned out to be far more serious, leading to a lengthy layoff and the cancellation of his move to Mainz. Since then, he has played with 30% vision in one eye, but has continued his career and it has not been a bad one. He has played for clubs such as Kaiserslautern, Coventry and Hearts, before moving to Spain, Indonesia and Malaysia, where he plays for Terengganu FC.

data.name: Joshua Brenet
data.description: Brenet once set a remarkable record during his time at PSV Eindhoven, going 53 consecutive league matches unbeaten. At the time he even made his Netherlands debut alongside Wesley Sneijder, Arjen Robben, Virgil van Dijk and Memphis Depay. He won three league titles and two Dutch Super Cups with PSV before moving to Hoffenheim, where he played under Julian Nagelsmann. Brenet fell out of favour after the future Germany coach left and he returned to the Netherlands to rejuvenate his career at Twente, before going abroad again to Qatar (Al-Rayyan), Scotland (Livingston) and Turkey (Kayserispor). The attacking full-back, known for his pace and technical ability, has been part of the Curaçao setup since 2024.

data.name: Tahith Chong
data.description: One of the few players in the setup actually born in Curaçao, the creative midfielder moved with his family from Willemstad to Rotterdam at the age of nine after being offered a place in the Feyenoord academy. Seven years later he moved again with his family – this time to England – after Manchester United offered him a place at their academy. He continued to develop and later trained with the first team under José Mourinho, who gave him his first senior appearance in a friendly. Competitive minutes followed under Ole Gunnar Solskjær, including a substitute appearance in the famous 3–1 comeback win at Paris Saint-Germain. “It’s been a special journey,” Chong told Voetbal International a few years ago. “My parents left everything behind in Curaçao for my dream.” Now the circle is complete with Chong back on the island playing for the national team while representing Sheffield United in the Championship.

data.name: Kevin Felida
data.description: Regarded as a huge prospect at the second-tier side Den Bosch, for whom he made more than 130 appearances before earning a transfer to RKC in the Eredivisie. However, the defensive midfielder did not break through there and returned to his former club. A ball-winning player with great stamina, he has been dubbed the “Bossche N’Golo Kanté”. One of his best friends in football is the Manchester United forward Joshua Zirkzee. Both grew up in Spijkenisse and spent a lot of time together, at and outside school. Just after Zirkzee had made his debut for Bayern Munich in December 2019, the two would meet up during the Christmas break to go for runs in the evenings. Felida made his debut for Curaçao in 2021 after receiving a call from the then head coach, Guus Hiddink, and eventually earned his first cap under Patrick Kluivert.

data.name: Riechedly Bazoer
data.description: One day in 2012 Bazoer was at the Manchester City training ground to have a look around their academy. “I went to Manchester with my brother, and the idea was that we would finalise everything there. We got a tour of the club, and head of academy Patrick Vieira came to speak to us, a real legend. It was all very impressive,” the defensive midfielder told Voetbal International. The move didn’t happen in the end as the most important person had not had her say: his mother. She felt her son would benefit from a more gradual development in the Netherlands, so Bazoer joined Ajax and worked under coaches such as Frank de Boer and Dennis Bergkamp. From there he moved on to Wolfsburg, Vitesse and AZ Alkmaar, before joining Konyaspor in 2024. After six friendly appearances for the Netherlands, he switched international allegiance to Curaçao last year.

data.name: Deveron Fonville
data.description: In 2024-25, Fonville was still playing amateur football with IJsselmeervogels, having arrived from fellow amateur side USV Hercules – who produced one of the biggest cup surprises in recent memory by beating Ajax in 2023. A product of the PSV Eindhoven and FC Utrecht academies, Fonville had to make back-up plans in case he never made it as a footballer. “I suddenly had to look beyond football and start working to earn money,” he told De Gelderlander. “I first worked in a warehouse and later as a recruitment consultant.” Everything changed last summer when he signed for FC Dordrecht. After just four appearances, he earned a move to NEC Nijmegen, the club where he had been on trial in 2023, but which fell through due to a registration issue. It completed a full circle – with two invaluable years of amateur football in between, which gave him senior football experience, before continuing to rise up the professional game.

data.name: Tyrick Bodak
data.description: After spells in the academies of PSV Eindhoven and De Graafschap, Bodak is now at Telstar but still awaiting his senior club debut. However, he already has several caps for Curaçao, having – unlike many of this teammates – risen through the country’s age groups to make his first-team debut at 19 while playing for PSV’s reserves and studying hotel and event management. “I’ve wanted to open my own bed & breakfast since secondary school,” he told TIO Business School, outlining his plans beyond football. Bodak even did an internship at his former teammate Mitch Apau’s chip shop, which is situated at the Telstar stadium, where Bodak is currently behind Ronald Koeman Jr in the pecking order. Sport runs in the family: his father and two of his brothers focused on baseball while another brother is also an active sportsman.

data.name: Trevor Doornbusch
data.description: When Trevor Doornbusch made his international debut in the summer of 2023, it came as something of a surprise as he had not featured in a single match for FC Dordrecht that season. As a youngster, he studied clips of Iker Casillas on YouTube and spent four years in the academy of Ajax before dropping into amateur football. His form earned him a move to Heerenveen and, although he never made the first team, he did represent the Netherlands at youth level during that time. Eventually, Doornbusch – known for being good with his feet and his long-range passing – had his breakthrough at Telstar, where he made his professional debut and continued his development as a modern goalkeeper.
